question.text: Compared to a regular plan, a direct plan of the same mutual fund scheme will have:

answer.text: A lower expense ratio and higher NAV
A higher expense ratio and lower NAV
The same expense ratio but higher NAV
A lower expense ratio but the same NAV

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** In a direct plan, the investor invests directly with the AMC without involving a distributor. Since no distributor commission is payable, the expense ratio of the direct plan is lower. Lower expenses mean more net assets per unit, resulting in a higher NAV compared to the regular plan of the same scheme. Over time, the difference in NAV between direct and regular plans widens.
**The Trap:** The NAV cannot be the same if expenses differ — lower expenses directly translate to higher NAV. The expense ratio of the direct plan is always lower (not higher) because the distributor trail commission is excluded.
**Cross-Exam Context:** MF topics overlap with Series V-A, V-B. Direct vs regular plan comparison is a very commonly tested concept, especially the impact on TER and NAV.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Be transparent with clients about the existence of direct plans. Your value as a distributor lies in advice, hand-holding, and service — not in hiding the cost difference. Clients who understand and appreciate your advice will stay with regular plans willingly.
